title: Summarization as Compression
concept: Using an LLM call to compress a long document or history into a dense summary before it enters context.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: why do LLM engineers bother compressing context before feeding it to a model?

options.text: To save tokens, cut cost, and avoid burying key info in a giant wall of text
Because models can only read text written in all lowercase letters
Because longer prompts always make the model refuse to answer

exercises.explain: Right! Compressing context keeps only what matters, which saves tokens (and money) and helps the model avoid 'context rot' — the tendency to lose track of important details in overly long inputs.

cards.title: 500 Pages? No Thanks.
cards.body: Imagine handing someone a 500-page report and saying 'answer my question using this.' They'd struggle to find the right part. Now imagine handing them a tight 1-page summary instead — much easier and faster.

cards.title: Summarization as Compression
cards.body: This means using an LLM call itself to shrink a long document or chat history into a short, dense summary. That summary — not the original wall of text — is what gets placed into the next prompt's context.

cards.title: The Meeting Notes Trick
cards.body: Think of a 2-hour meeting. Nobody replays the whole recording to remember what happened — someone writes meeting notes. Those notes are the compressed version: shorter, but they keep the decisions and key facts.

cards.title: Chatbot With a Long History
cards.body: A customer support bot has had 40 back-and-forth messages with a user. Instead of resending all 40 messages every time, the app periodically calls the LLM to summarize: 'User's order #1234 arrived damaged, wants a refund, already provided photo proof.' That short summary replaces the raw history in future prompts.

cards.title: Myth: Summarizing Always Loses What Matters
cards.body: Good summarization is targeted — it keeps facts, decisions, and numbers relevant to the task, and drops small talk or repeated phrasing. It's not just 'making text shorter randomly,' it's compressing while protecting the important signal.

cards.title: Raw History vs. Summarized History
cards.body: Same information, very different token cost and clarity.

left.text: 40 messages, 6,000 tokens, greetings and small talk mixed with key facts

right.text: 5 sentences, 80 tokens, only order number, issue, and requested action

exercises.prompt: What is the core idea of using summarization for context compression?

options.text: Use an LLM call to turn a long document or history into a short, dense summary that goes into context instead of the full text
Delete random sentences from the document until it fits the token limit
Translate the document into a different language to make it shorter
Ask the user to manually shorten their own message before sending it

exercises.explain: Summarization compression means calling the LLM itself to intelligently condense long text or conversation history into a short summary, which is what actually gets fed into the next prompt — much better than random deletion.

exercises.prompt: True or False: When summarizing chat history for context compression, the goal is to keep every word exactly the same but just delete a few sentences.
exercises.explain: False. Summarization isn't about keeping the same wording and trimming a bit — it's about rewriting the content into a denser form that preserves key facts while dropping filler, small talk, and repetition.

exercises.sentence: Instead of resending the full 40-message chat log, the app calls the LLM to create a short summary that replaces the raw history in the next prompt's context.
exercises.explain: The whole point of this technique is generating a summary — a compressed, information-dense stand-in for the original long text.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is most likely an AI-generated summary used for context compression (versus a human just chatting)?

options.text: "User purchased Plan B on 3/14, reported login failure on 3/16, was issued a temporary password, issue unresolved as of last message."
"lol yeah so basically I tried logging in like three times and it just wouldn't work, so annoying, anyway thanks for the help earlier!"
"Hey! Just wanted to say thanks again for your patience, hope you have a great rest of your day!"

exercises.explain: Option A reads like a structured, fact-dense summary — dates, actions, status — exactly what an LLM produces when asked to compress a conversation into key details. The other two are casual, conversational human messages, not summaries.

exercises.prompt: Your app has a customer support agent that's been chatting with a user for 2 hours across 60 messages. Tokens are getting expensive and the model keeps forgetting earlier details. What's the best fix using what you've learned so far?

options.text: Periodically call the LLM to summarize the conversation into key facts and decisions, and use that summary instead of the full 60-message log in future prompts
Keep sending all 60 messages every time so nothing is ever lost
Stop the conversation and tell the user to start over from scratch
Randomly delete every other message to save space

exercises.explain: Summarizing periodically gives you the best of both worlds: it drastically cuts token count (saving cost) while preserving the important facts and decisions, which also reduces the risk of context rot from an overly long history. Sending everything (b) is expensive and risks context rot; ending the chat (c) breaks the user experience; and random deletion (d) is likely to throw away exactly the details you need. This is why summarization is one of the most common and reliable compression techniques in real LLM applications.
